hangulBasic.title: Hangul Basics
hangulBasic.content:
### **Lesson 1: Hangul Basics** 📝

The Hangul alphabet (**한글**) is one of the world's most logical writing systems. Created in 1443 by King Sejong the Great, it uses features—each letter's shape represents the mouth position when making that sound.

#### 1. Basic Consonants (14)

| Jamo | Sound | Example |
|------|-------|---------|
| ㄱ | g/k | `<speak='가'>` 가 |
| ㄴ | n | `<speak='나'>` 나 |
| ㄷ | d/t | `<speak='다'>` 다 |
| ㄹ | r/l | `<speak='라'>` 라 |
| ㅁ | m | `<speak='마'>` 마 |
| ㅂ | b/p | `<speak='바'>` 바 |
| ㅅ | s | `<speak='사'>` 사 |
| ㅇ | silent (start) / ng (end) | `<speak='아'>` 아 |
| ㅈ | j | `<speak='자'>` 자 |
| ㅊ | ch | `<speak='차'>` 차 |
| ㅋ | k (aspirated) | `<speak='카'>` 카 |
| ㅌ | t (aspirated) | `<speak='타'>` 타 |
| ㅍ | p (aspirated) | `<speak='파'>` 파 |
| ㅎ | h | `<speak='하'>` 하 |

#### 2. Basic Vowels (10)

| Jamo | Sound | Example |
|------|-------|----------|
| ㅏ | a | `<speak='아'>` 아 |
| ㅓ | eo | `<speak='어'>` 어 |
| ㅗ | o | `<speak='오'>` 오 |
| ㅜ | u | `<speak='우'>` 우 |
| ㅡ | eu | `<speak='으'>` 으 |
| ㅣ | i | `<speak='이'>` 이 |
| ㅐ | ae | `<speak='애'>` 애 |
| ㅔ | e | `<speak='에'>` 에 |
| ㅑ | ya | `<speak='야'>` 야 |
| ㅕ | yeo | `<speak='여'>` 여 |

#### 3. How to Form Syllables

In Hangul, characters combine into **syllabic blocks**. Each complete syllable (음절) needs:
- An **initial consonant** (초성)
- A **vowel** (중성)
- An optional **final consonant** or consonant cluster (종성/받침)

**Reading order**: top-to-bottom, left-to-right.

**Examples**:
- 가 `<speak='가'>` = ㄱ + ㅏ
- 아 `<speak='아'>` = ㅇ + ㅏ (silent initial)
- 담 `<speak='담'>` = ㄷ + ㅏ + ㅁ (with **batchim**)

#### 4. Double Vowels (Complex)

Some vowels are combinations—pronounced as a single compressed sound, not separate:
- ㅘ (wa) `<speak='와'>`
- ㅙ (wae) `<speak='왜'>`
- ㅚ (oe) `<speak='외'>`
- ㅝ (wo) `<speak='워'>`
- ㅞ (we) `<speak='웨'>`
- ㅟ (wi) `<speak='위'>`
- ㅢ (ui) `<speak='의'>`

#### Practice: Read These

- 바 `<speak='바'>`  
- 저 `<speak='저'>`  
- 미 `<speak='미'>`  
- 토 `<speak='토'>`  
- 하 `<speak='하'>`  
- 표 `<speak='표'>`

**The Hangul Master Key:**  
Master these 24 base characters (14 consonants + 10 vowels), and you can read almost any Korean word!

batchim.title: Batchim (Final Consonants)
batchim.content:
### **Lesson 1.1: The Secret of Batchim (받침)** 🌀

**Batchim** is the final consonant of a syllable. Even though Korean uses many letters, at the end of a syllable, they only result in **7 possible sounds**.

#### 1. The 7 Golden Rules

| Sound | Letters | Example |
|:---:|---------|---------|
| **K** | ㄱ, ㅋ, ㄲ | `<speak='읽다'>` 읽다 (Read) |
| **N** | ㄴ | `<speak='안'>` 안 (Inside) |
| **T** | ㄷ, ㅅ, ㅆ, ㅈ, ㅊ, ㅌ, ㅎ | `<speak='꽃'>` 꽃 (Flower) |
| **L** | ㄹ | `<speak='물'>` 물 (Water) |
| **M** | ㅁ | `<speak='엄마'>` 엄마 (Mom) |
| **P** | ㅂ, ㅍ | `<speak='입'>` 입 (Mouth) |
| **NG** | ㅇ | `<speak='강'>` 강 (River) |

#### 2. Linking Rule
If the next syllable starts with a vowel (**ㅇ**), the final consonant "jumps" to the next syllable.
Example: `<speak='꽃이'>` 꽃이 (The flower) sounds like "ko-chi".

colors.title: Colors
colors.content:
### **Lesson 2: Colors (색깔)** 🎨

In Korean, the word for color is **색 (saek)** or **색깔 (saek-kkal)**. Many times, we add "saek" to the name of the color to identify it clearly.

#### 1. Main Colors List

| Color | Korean | Pronunciation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Red | 빨간색 | `<speak='빨간색'>` |
| Blue | 파란색 | `<speak='파란색'>` |
| Yellow | 노란색 | `<speak='노란색'>` |
| Green | 초록색 | `<speak='초록색'>` |
| Black | 검정색 | `<speak='검정색'>` |
| White | 하얀색 | `<speak='하얀색'>` |

#### 2. Grammatical Curiosity
Colors in Korean can act as nouns or adjectives. 
* **Noun:** "I like blue" (파란색을 좋아해요).
* **Adjective:** When the color ends in **ㄴ** (like 빨간), it's already ready to describe something immediately: **빨간 사과** (Red apple).

numbers.title: Numbers (Sino-Korean and Native)
numbers.content:
### **Lesson 3: Numbers** 🔢

Korean has two number systems. Using the right one is key!

#### 1. Sino-Korean (Money, dates, minutes)
Use for **Money (원)** and calculations.
- `<speak='일'>` 일 (1), `<speak='이'>` 이 (2), `<speak='삼'>` 삼 (3), `<speak='십'>` 십 (10).

#### 2. Native Korean (Counting items, age, hours)
Use for items in your inventory.
- `<speak='하나'>` 하나 (1), `<speak='둘'>` 둘 (2), `<speak='셋'>` 셋 (3), `<speak='열'>` 열 (10).

**Note:** Before counters, 1, 2, 3, 4 change to **한, 두, 세, 네**.

ordinalNumbers.title: Ordinal Numbers + Advanced Quantifiers
ordinalNumbers.content:
### **Lesson 4: Ordinal Numbers** 📊

To indicate order (first, second...), we use specific prefixes or suffixes.

| Order | Korean | Audio |
|-------|---------|-------|
| First | 첫 | `<speak='첫'>` |
| Second | 둘째 | `<speak='둘째'>` |
| Third | 셋째 | `<speak='셋째'>` |
| Fourth | 넷째 | `<speak='넷째'>` |
| Fifth | 다섯째 | `<speak='다섯째'>` |

bodyParts.title: Body Parts
bodyParts.content:
### **Lesson 5: Body Parts** 👤

Knowing body parts is vital to understand where a monster attacked or which equipment to equip!

#### 1. Essential Vocabulary

| Body Part | Korean | Pronunciation and Audio |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Head / Hair | 머리 | `<speak='머리'>` (meo-ri) |
| Eye | 눈 | `<speak='눈'>` (nun) |
| Nose | 코 | `<speak='코'>` (ko) |
| Mouth | 입 | `<speak='입'>` (ip) |
| Ear / Hearing | 귀 | `<speak='귀'>` (gwi) |
| Hand | 손 | `<speak='손'>` (son) |
| Foot | 발 | `<speak='발'>` (bal) |
| Body (Whole) | 몸 | `<speak='몸'>` (mom) |

> **Game Curiosity:** The word 눈 `<speak='눈'>` means both "eye" and "snow". Context (or game biome) defines which one!

animals.title: Animals
animals.content:
### **Lesson 6: Creatures and Animals** 🐕

#### 1. Common Animals

| Animal | Korean | Pronunciation and Audio |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dog | 개 | `<speak='개'>` (gae) |
| Cat | 고양이 | `<speak='고양이'>` (go-yang-i) |
| Bird | 새 | `<speak='새'>` (sae) |
| Fish | 물고기 | `<speak='물고기'>` (mul-go-gi) |
| Rabbit | 토끼 | `<speak='토끼'>` (to-kki) |
| Bear | 곰 | `<speak='곰'>` (gom) |
| Tiger | 호랑이 | `<speak='호랑이'>` (ho-rang-i) |

*Dica:* Remember the counter for animals from Lesson 4? To say "Three bears", you join the noun + number + counter: **곰 세 마리** `<speak='곰 세 마리'>`.

family.title: Family and Kinship
family.content:
### **Lesson 7: Family and Kinship Terms** 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦

Korean culture is very based on hierarchy. The way you call someone depends on **your** gender and the other person's age.

#### 1. Basic Terms
* **Mother:** 엄마 `<speak='엄마'>` (eom-ma - casual) / 어머니 (eo-meo-ni - formal)
* **Father:** 아빠 `<speak='아빠'>` (a-ppa - casual) / 아버지 (a-beo-ji - formal)
* **Younger Sibling (independent of your gender):** 동생 `<speak='동생'>` (dong-saeng)

#### 2. Older Siblings (The Gender Trap)
| If you are MAN calling: | Term | If you are WOMAN calling: | Term |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Older Brother | **형 (Hyeong)** `<speak='형'>` | Older Brother | **오빠 (Oppa)** `<speak='오빠'>` |
| Older Sister | **누나 (Nuna)** `<speak='누나'>` | Older Sister | **언니 (Eonni)** `<speak='언니'>` |

foodDrinks.title: Food and Drinks
foodDrinks.content:
### **Lesson 8: Food and Drinks** 🍎

#### 1. Item Vocabulary
* **Water:** 물 `<speak='물'>` (mul)
* **Food/Rice:** 밥 `<speak='밥'>` (bap)
* **Bread:** 빵 `<speak='빵'>` (ppang)
* **Meat:** 고기 `<speak='고기'>` (go-gi)
* **Vegetables:** 야채 `<speak='야채'>` (ya-chae)

#### 2. Essential Adjective
* **Tasty:** 맛있다 `<speak='맛있다'>` (mas-it-da)
* Conjugated: **맛있어요!** `<speak='맛있어요'>` (It's tasty!)

clothing.title: Clothing
clothing.content:
### **Lesson 9: Clothing** 👕

- `<speak='옷'>` 옷 (Clothes)
- `<speak='셔츠'>` 셔츠 (Shirt)
- `<speak='바지'>` 바지 (Pants)
- `<speak='치마'>` 치마 (Skirt)
- `<speak='신발'>` 신발 (Shoes)
- `<speak='모자'>` 모자 (Hat)
- `<speak='지갑'>` 지갑 (Wallet)

householdItems.content:

schoolSupplies.title: School and School Supplies
schoolSupplies.content:
### **Lesson 11: School Supplies** 📚

- `<speak='학교'>` 학교 (School)
- `<speak='공책'>` 공책 (Notebook)
- `<speak='펜'>` 펜 (Pen)
- `<speak='연필'>` 연필 (Pencil)
- `<speak='지우개'>` 지우개 (Eraser)
- `<speak='선생님'>` 선생님 (Teacher)
- `<speak='학생'>` 학생 (Student)

emotions.title: Emotions and Feelings
emotions.content:
### **Lesson 12: Emotions and Feelings** 🎭

#### 1. Feeling Verbs (Conjugated in 해요체)
* **Happy:** 행복해요 `<speak='행복해요'>`
* **Sad:** 슬퍼요 `<speak='슬퍼요'>`
* **Angry:** 화나요 `<speak='화나요'>`
* **Scared:** 무서워요 `<speak='무서워요'>` (Irregular ㅂ -> 우)

weather.title: Weather and Atmospheric Conditions
weather.content:
### **Lesson 13: Weather** 🌤️

- `<speak='날씨'>` 날씨 (Weather)
- `<speak='비'>` 비 (Rain)
- `<speak='눈'>` 눈 (Snow)
- `<speak='바람'>` 바람 (Wind)
- `<speak='구름'>` 구름 (Cloud)
- `<speak='해'>` 해 (Sun)
- `<speak='달'>` 달 (Moon)

daysOfWeek.title: Days of the Week
daysOfWeek.content:
### **Lesson 14 & 15: Days of the Week and Months** 📅

#### 1. Days of the Week (All end in 요일)

| Day | Korean | Pronunciation |
|-----|--------|---------------|
| Monday | 월요일 | `<speak='월요일'>` |
| Tuesday | 화요일 | `<speak='화요일'>` |
| Wednesday | 수요일 | `<speak='수요일'>` |
| Thursday | 목요일 | `<speak='목요일'>` |
| Friday | 금요일 | `<speak='금요일'>` |
| Saturday | 토요일 | `<speak='토요일'>` |
| Sunday | 일요일 | `<speak='일요일'>` |

#### 2. Months of the Year

| Month | Korean | Pronunciation |
|-------|--------|---------------|
| January | 1월 | `<speak='일월'>` |
| February | 2월 | `<speak='이월'>` |
| March | 3월 | `<speak='삼월'>` |
| April | 4월 | `<speak='사월'>` |
| May | 5월 | `<speak='오월'>` |
| June | 6월 | `<speak='유월'>` |
| July | 7월 | `<speak='칠월'>` |
| August | 8월 | `<speak='팔월'>` |
| September | 9월 | `<speak='구월'>` |
| October | 10월 | `<speak='시월'>` |
| November | 11월 | `<speak='십일월'>` |
| December | 12월 | `<speak='십이월'>` |

months.title: Months of the Year
months.content: See Days of the Week lesson for months content.

timeOfDay.title: Hours and Time of Day
timeOfDay.content:
### **Lesson 16: Time of Day** ⏰

- `<speak='아침'>` 아침 (Morning)
- `<speak='낮'>` 낮 (Day)
- `<speak='저녁'>` 저녁 (Evening)
- `<speak='밤'>` 밤 (Night)
- `<speak='자정'>` 자정 (Midnight)
- `<speak='정오'>` 정오 (Noon)

directions.title: Directions and Location
directions.content:
### **Lesson 18: Directions and Location** 🗺️

To indicate location, we use the particle **에** (e).

| Position | Korean | Audio |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Above | 위 | `<speak='위'>` |
| Below | 아래 | `<speak='아래'>` |
| Front | 앞 | `<speak='앞'>` |
| Behind | 뒤 | `<speak='뒤'>` |
| Side | 옆 | `<speak='옆'>` |
| Inside | 안 | `<speak='안'>` |
| Outside | 밖 | `<speak='밖'>` |

**Example:** Inside the box **안에** `<speak='상자 안에'>` (Inside the box).

basicVerbs.title: Basic Verbs + Conjugation (해요체)
basicVerbs.content:
### **Lesson 19: Basic Verbs + Conjugation (해요체)** 🏃

The sentence structure in Korean is different: **Subject + Object + VERB**. The verb or adjective always goes at the **end** of the sentence! 

In the dictionary, all verbs end in **다** (da). To use them in everyday life with polite standard style (요 style), we need to conjugate.

#### 1. How to find the verb root?
Just remove the **다**. 
* 먹다 (Eat) -> Root: **먹**
* 가다 (Go) -> Root: **가**

#### 2. The 3 Magic Rules of Present (해요체)

**Rule 1: If the last vowel of the root is ㅏ or ㅗ, add 아요.**
* 가다 (Go) -> 가 + 아요 = **가요** `<speak='가요'>` (I go)
* 보다 (See) -> 보 + 아요 = **봐요** `<speak='봐요'>` (I see - ㅗ + ㅏ merge into ㅘ)

**Rule 2: If the last vowel is any other (ㅓ, ㅜ, ㅡ, ㅣ), add 어요.**
* 먹다 (Eat) -> 먹 + 어요 = **먹어요** `<speak='먹어요'>` (I eat)
* 읽다 (Read) -> 읽 + 어요 = **읽어요** `<speak='읽어요'>` (I read)

**Rule 3: The verb 하다 (Do).**
* 하다 is irregular and always becomes **해요** `<speak='해요'>` (I do).
* 공부하다 (Study) -> **공부해요** `<speak='공부해요'>` (I study).

basicAdjectives.title: Basic Adjectives and the Modifier
basicAdjectives.content:
### **Lesson 20: Basic Adjectives and the Modifier** ✨

In Korean, adjectives are called **"Descriptive Verbs"**. They end in **다** and conjugate exactly like action verbs! (Ex: 크다 -> 커요 = It's big).

But how do we use an adjective before a noun, like "Big Sword"? Here enters the **Modifier** system.

#### 1. Transforming the Adjective (The Modifier Rule)
To "glue" an adjective directly to a noun, add **~(으)ㄴ** to the adjective root.

* **If the root ends in a vowel (No Batchim):** Add **ㄴ** below.
  * 크다 (Big) -> 큰 (Big)
  * 작다 (Small) -> 작은 (Small)

* **If the root ends in a consonant (With Batchim):** Add **은**.
  * 좋다 (Good) -> 좋은 (Good)
  * 나쁘다 (Bad) -> 나쁜 (Bad)

#### 2. Initial Adjectives Vocabulary

| Adjective (Dictionary) | Meaning | As Modifier |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 크다 | To be big | 큰 (Big) |
| 작다 | To be small | 작은 (Small) |
| 많다 | To have much/Many | 많은 (Many/Much) |
| 적다 | To have little/Few | 적은 (Few) |
| 좋다 | To be good | 좋은 (Good) |
| 나쁘다 | To be bad | 나쁜 (Bad) |

banmal.title: Banmal
banmal.content:
### **Lesson 21: Banmal (Casual Speech)** 🧢

**Banmal** is used with friends or people younger than you.

- `<speak='너'>` 너 (You - casual)
- `<speak='나'>` 나 (I - casual)

**Rule:** Generally, you just remove the polite suffix **요** (e.g., 가요 -> 가).

simpleParticles.title: Simple Particles
simpleParticles.content:
### **Lesson 22: Simple Particles (은/는, 이/가, 을/를)** ⚙️

Particles indicate what function each word has in the sentence.

1.  **Topic (은/는):** Indicates what you're talking about.
    * Ends in Vowel? **는**. Ex: 사과는... (About the apple...)
    * Ends in Consonant? **은**. Ex: 책은... (About the book...)
2.  **Subject (이/가):** Indicates who performs the action.
    * Vowel? **가**. Ex: 머리가... (The head...)
    * Consonant? **이**. Ex: 책이... (The book...)
3.  **Object (을/를):** Indicates what suffers the verb's action.
    * Vowel? **를**. Ex: 사과를... (The apple...)
    * Consonant? **을**. Ex: 책을... (The book...)

opposites.title: Opposites and Contraries
opposites.content:
### **Lesson 23: Opposites** 🔀

- `<speak='크다'>` 크다 (Big) / `<speak='작다'>` 작다 (Small)
- `<speak='높다'>` 높다 (High) / `<speak='낮다'>` 낮다 (Low)
- `<speak='많다'>` 많다 (Many) / `<speak='적다'>` 적다 (Few)

appearance.title: Describing Appearance
appearance.content:
### **Lesson 24: Appearance** 🎨

- `<speak='아름답다'>` 아름답다 (Beautiful)
- `<speak='예쁘다'>` 예쁘다 (Pretty)
- `<speak='잘생기다'>` 잘생기다 (Handsome)
- `<speak='강하다'>` 강하다 (Strong)
- `<speak='약하다'>` 약하다 (Weak)

dailyRoutine.title: Daily Routine
dailyRoutine.content:
### **Lesson 25: Daily Routine** 🔄

- `<speak='깨어나다'>` 깨어나다 (Wake up)
- `<speak='씻다'>` 씻다 (Wash)
- `<speak='돌아오다'>` 돌아오다 (Return)
- `<speak='숙제를 하다'>` 숙제를 하다 (Do homework)

basicQuestions.title: Basic Questions
basicQuestions.content:
### **Lesson 26: Basic Questions** ❓

- `<speak='누구'>` 누구 (Who?)
- `<speak='뭐'>` 뭐 (What?)
- `<speak='어디'>` 어디 (Where?)
- `<speak='언제'>` 언제 (When?)
- `<speak='왜'>` 왜 (Why?)

hasipsioche.title: Hasipsioche
hasipsioche.content:
### **Lesson 28: Formal Style (Hasipsioche)** 👑

Used in formal situations or public speaking.

- Ends in **~습니다** (statement) or **~습니까?** (question).
- Example: `<speak='사람입니다'>` (It is a person).

geography.title: Geography and Nature
geography.content:
### **Lesson 29: Geography** 🌍

- `<speak='산'>` 산 (Mountain)
- `<speak='강'>` 강 (River)
- `<speak='바다'>` 바다 (Sea)
- `<speak='섬'>` 섬 (Island)
- `<speak='사막'>` 사막 (Desert)

professions.title: Professions
professions.content:
### **Lesson 30: Professions** 💼

- `<speak='의사'>` 의사 (Doctor)
- `<speak='경찰관'>` 경찰관 (Police officer)
- `<speak='요리사'>` 요리사 (Cook)
- `<speak='가수'>` 가수 (Singer)
- `<speak='화가'>` 화가 (Painter)

toysGames.title: Toys, Games and Hobbies
toysGames.content:
### **Lesson 31: Toys and Games** 🎮

- `<speak='공'>` 공 (Ball)
- `<speak='인형'>` 인형 (Doll)
- `<speak='장난감'>` 장난감 (Toy)
- `<speak='퍼즐'>` 퍼즐 (Puzzle)

transport.title: Transportation
transport.content:
### **Lesson 32: Transportation** 🚗

- `<speak='자동차'>` 자동차 (Car)
- `<speak='기차'>` 기차 (Train)
- `<speak='버스'>` 버스 (Bus)
- `<speak='비행기'>` 비행기 (Airplane)
- `<speak='배'>` 배 (Boat)

koreanFood.title: Typical Korean Food
koreanFood.content:
### **Lesson 34: Korean Food** 🍲

- `<speak='김치'>` 김치 (Kimchi)
- `<speak='비빔밥'>` 비빔밥 (Bibimbap)
- `<speak='불고기'>` 불고기 (Bulgogi)
- `<speak='떡볶이'>` 떡볶이 (Tteokbokki)

culturalHolidays.title: Holidays and Korean Culture
culturalHolidays.content:
### **Lesson 35: Culture** 🎆

- `<speak='설날'>` 설날 (Lunar New Year)
- `<speak='추석'>` 추석 (Chuseok)
- `<speak='생일'>` 생일 (Birthday)
- `<speak='축제'>` 축제 (Festival)

teacher.title: 🎓 Teacher
teacher.fullHealthRequired: You must be at full health to study!
teacher.notEnoughWords: Not enough words learned yet to take the test!
teacher.vocabNote: The teacher will now send harder vocabulary!
teacher.writeEmoji: Write the name of the object:
teacher.conjugationPrompt: What is the correct conjugation of:
teacher.difficultyWarning: ⚠️ Consulting the teacher expands your vocabulary — but also makes the game harder!
teacher.lessonOnCooldown: You have no lessons for now
teacher.lessonCooldownNote: Come back later — lessons recharge over time

tutorial.typeToKill: Type the word to defeat the monster
tutorial.typeToNavigate: Type a direction to move to the next room
tutorial.typeToOpen: Type the word to open the {{room}}
tutorial.typeToTalk: Type the word to talk to the {{room}}
tutorial.typeToBuy: Type the word to buy from the {{room}}
tutorial.pressMap: Press M to view the map and your location
tutorial.findBoss: Find the boss room to increase the challenge
tutorial.buyTent: You could buy a tent at the shop to sleep through the night
tutorial.pickHangul: Type the syllable on the coin to pick up an item
tutorial.pickHanja: Type the hanja syllable to pick up an item — or use a Dictionary to reveal it
tutorial.typeToAdvance: Type the word to advance to the next world
tutorial.findTeacher: Finding the teacher can teach you a lesson and increase the challenge
tutorial.casinoLuck: Why not try your luck risking it all?

donate.body: You've opened the game {{count}} times… Consider supporting my work — I believe in free educational platforms and people like you can help advance this platform.

ttsWarning.body: Your browser doesn't support Korean text-to-speech. For the best experience, Google Chrome is recommended.

credits.thanks: Special thanks to Roh HaYoung and KIT for showing me Korean culture and language, I'm eternally grateful to all of you. You have changed my life forever. I hope this project inspires more people to study your language and connect to your beautiful and inspiring culture.
